items.prompt_md: Why does the maximum product subarray recurrence track a running minimum in addition to a running maximum?
options: Because a negative nums[i] can turn the running minimum into the new running maximum
Because the minimum is needed to detect when the subarray should reset to empty
Because Python's max() function requires a minimum bound argument
Because products can never be negative, so the minimum is just a sanity check
items.answer: Because a negative nums[i] can turn the running minimum into the new running maximum
items.explanation_md: Multiplying a negative running minimum by a negative nums[i] produces a large positive value, so the new max might come from the old min, not the old max.

items.prompt_md: At each step of the max-product-subarray scan, the new cur_max and cur_min are computed as the max and min of which three candidates?
options: nums[i] alone, cur_max * nums[i], and cur_min * nums[i]
nums[i] alone, cur_max + nums[i], and cur_min + nums[i]
Only cur_max * nums[i] and cur_min * nums[i], never nums[i] alone
The average of cur_max and cur_min, multiplied by nums[i]
items.answer: nums[i] alone, cur_max * nums[i], and cur_min * nums[i]
items.explanation_md: Starting fresh at nums[i], extending the running max, and extending the running min are the only three ways the best product ending at i could arise.

items.prompt_md: Why must `nums[i]` alone be one of the three candidates considered at each step, rather than always extending the previous running product?
options: Because if the running product so far is unhelpful (e.g. very negative and about to be multiplied by a positive number), starting a fresh subarray at i can beat extending
Because nums[i] alone is always the largest of the three candidates
Because the array might not contain any negative numbers
Because Python requires an explicit base case for every loop iteration
items.answer: Because if the running product so far is unhelpful (e.g. very negative and about to be multiplied by a positive number), starting a fresh subarray at i can beat extending
items.explanation_md: This is the same 'restart or extend' choice that Kadane's max-subarray-sum makes, just carried out for both the running max and running min.

items.prompt_md: In Kadane's algorithm for maximum subarray SUM (not product), what is the recurrence for dp[i], the best sum of a subarray ending at index i?
options: dp[i] = max(nums[i], nums[i] + dp[i-1])
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]) + nums[i]
dp[i] = nums[i] * dp[i-1]
dp[i] = min(nums[i], nums[i] + dp[i-1])
items.answer: dp[i] = max(nums[i], nums[i] + dp[i-1])
items.explanation_md: At each index you either start a new subarray at i or extend the previous best sum by adding nums[i], taking whichever is larger.

items.prompt_md: Why does Kadane's max-subarray-sum only need a single running max, while max-product-subarray needs both a running max and a running min?
options: Addition can't flip sign the way multiplication by a negative number can, so a low running sum never suddenly becomes the best sum
Sums are always non-negative, so there is nothing to track a minimum for
Kadane's algorithm is fundamentally a different problem with no shared structure
Products require two variables purely for performance reasons, not correctness
items.answer: Addition can't flip sign the way multiplication by a negative number can, so a low running sum never suddenly becomes the best sum
items.explanation_md: The running-minimum trick exists specifically because multiplying two negatives produces a positive, an effect addition simply doesn't have.

items.prompt_md: How should the running max and running min be reset if the array contains a zero at index i?
options: Both cur_max and cur_min should reset to 0 (or nums[i], which is 0), since any product crossing the zero is wiped out
Only cur_max should reset to 0; cur_min should keep its old value
Zeros should be skipped entirely and excluded from the array before scanning
Zeros have no special effect and require no reset at all
items.answer: Both cur_max and cur_min should reset to 0 (or nums[i], which is 0), since any product crossing the zero is wiped out
items.explanation_md: Multiplying by zero collapses any running product to zero, so both extremes effectively restart from that point.

items.prompt_md: What is the most common bug when implementing max-product-subarray from scratch?
options: Updating cur_max using the already-updated cur_min instead of the previous iteration's cur_min
Forgetting to initialize best to negative infinity
Using a for loop instead of a while loop
Returning cur_min instead of best at the end
items.answer: Updating cur_max using the already-updated cur_min instead of the previous iteration's cur_min
items.explanation_md: Because cur_max and cur_min both depend on each other's previous value, computing candidates before overwriting either variable avoids using a value from the wrong timestep.

items.prompt_md: In the lesson's max-product-subarray code, why is `best` updated with `max(best, cur_max)` rather than `max(best, cur_max, cur_min)`?
options: Because the overall best product can only ever come from a running maximum at some position, never from a running minimum directly
Because cur_min is always larger than cur_max at every step
Because best only needs to track the smallest value seen
Because cur_min is not used anywhere in the algorithm
items.answer: Because the overall best product can only ever come from a running maximum at some position, never from a running minimum directly
items.explanation_md: cur_min exists only to feed future cur_max computations when a negative number flips it; the final answer is always drawn from the max values seen at each step.

items.prompt_md: Both house-robber and max-product-subarray have a 'Fibonacci-shaped lookback' where only index i-1 matters. What distinguishes them?
options: House-robber carries one rolling value forward; max-product-subarray must carry two (max and min) because of the sign-flip risk from multiplication
House-robber requires scanning every earlier index, while max-product-subarray only looks at i-1
They are identical problems with different variable names
Max-product-subarray does not use dynamic programming at all
items.answer: House-robber carries one rolling value forward; max-product-subarray must carry two (max and min) because of the sign-flip risk from multiplication
items.explanation_md: The lookback window is the same (just i-1), but the amount of state carried differs: one scalar suffices for a sum-like problem, two are needed once sign flips are possible.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass max-product-subarray solution that tracks a running max and min?
options: O(n) time, O(1) space
O(n) time, O(n) space
O(n^2) time, O(1) space
O(n log n) time, O(1) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(1) space
items.explanation_md: One pass over the array, two rolling scalars — O(n) time, O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a brute-force approach that computes the product of every contiguous subarray from scratch?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: There are O(n^2) contiguous subarrays, and even computing each product incrementally still costs O(n^2) total to check them all.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of Kadane's algorithm for maximum subarray SUM?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Kadane's is a single linear pass carrying one rolling value forward, giving O(n) time and O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: If you naively recomputed cur_max and cur_min by re-scanning from the start of the array at every index i (instead of rolling them forward), what would the resulting time complexity become?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Re-deriving the running extremes from scratch at each of n positions, each scan costing O(n), gives O(n^2) total — exactly what the rolling-variable approach avoids.

items.prompt_md: You need the largest product of any contiguous subarray, where elements can be negative, positive, or zero.
options: Track two rolling values per position — a running max and a running min — since a negative element can flip which one becomes the new best
Track a single running max, exactly like maximum subarray sum
Sort the array and multiply the largest contiguous run of positives
Use a hash map of prefix products and look up repeats
items.answer: Track two rolling values per position — a running max and a running min — since a negative element can flip which one becomes the new best
items.explanation_md: A single running max fails the moment a negative number appears, because it can turn the smallest running product into the largest one — both extremes must be carried forward.

items.prompt_md: You need the largest SUM of any contiguous subarray of an integer array, which may contain negative numbers.
options: 1-D DP over the array (Kadane's): dp[i] = max(nums[i], nums[i] + dp[i-1]), tracked with a single rolling variable
Track a running max AND a running min, exactly like the product version
Sort the array and sum the largest contiguous run
Two pointers expanding a window from both ends
items.answer: 1-D DP over the array (Kadane's): dp[i] = max(nums[i], nums[i] + dp[i-1]), tracked with a single rolling variable
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: addition can't flip sign the way multiplication can, so unlike the product version, a single running max is enough here — tracking a min too would be unnecessary extra state.

items.prompt_md: An array of positive integers represents house values along a street, and you must rob the maximum total value without robbing two adjacent houses.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]), a fixed two-step lookback
Track a running max AND running min, since some house values could be considered 'negative' in effect
Sort the houses by value and greedily take the largest ones first
Two pointers, one at each end of the street
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]), a fixed two-step lookback
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: house-robber's adjacency constraint needs a fixed lookback of two positions, not the running-max-and-min trick that only matters when multiplication can flip sign.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of positive integers only (no zeros, no negatives), find the maximum product of any contiguous subarray.
options: A single running max is enough, since with no negatives or zeros the product only grows as the subarray extends and there is nothing to flip
You must track a running max AND running min regardless, since the problem says 'product'
Sort the array first, since sorting changes which subarrays are contiguous
Use backtracking to try every subarray boundary
items.answer: A single running max is enough, since with no negatives or zeros the product only grows as the subarray extends and there is nothing to flip
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: the running-min trick exists specifically to handle sign flips from negative numbers; if the input is guaranteed all-positive, tracking a minimum adds no value.

items.prompt_md: You must find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray, but the array is extremely large and sorted in non-decreasing order, and you only need to check whether the maximum sum exceeds a given threshold.
options: 1-D DP (Kadane's) over the array with a single rolling max, since the sorted order doesn't change the definition of 'contiguous subarray'
Binary search over the sorted array to find the threshold's position
Track a running max and running min like the product version
Two pointers converging from both ends of the sorted array
items.answer: 1-D DP (Kadane's) over the array with a single rolling max, since the sorted order doesn't change the definition of 'contiguous subarray'
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: sortedness is a red herring here, because 'contiguous subarray' refers to the original index order, not sorted order — binary search doesn't apply, and Kadane's still governs.

items.prompt_md: Given an array that may contain negative numbers, find the maximum product of any contiguous subarray, then report the actual start and end indices of that subarray, not just its value.
options: Track running max and min values AND the indices where each was last reset, updating them alongside the numeric rolling state
Track only the numeric running max and min, since indices can always be recovered afterward with no extra state
Sort the array and binary search for the subarray boundaries
Use backtracking to enumerate every subarray and its indices
items.answer: Track running max and min values AND the indices where each was last reset, updating them alongside the numeric rolling state
items.explanation_md: Still the same running-max/running-min DP shape, but reconstructing the actual subarray means carrying extra bookkeeping (start index) alongside the numeric rolling values.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine if a given contiguous subarray product target is achievable at all (yes/no), and the array is small enough (n <= 15) that trying every subarray combination directly is acceptable.
options: Backtracking / brute force over subarray boundaries, since the tiny bound makes exhaustive checking acceptable and it's a yes/no existence question rather than an optimization
Track a running max and running min to find the optimum product
Binary search over possible target values
Use a stack to track sign changes in the subarray
items.answer: Backtracking / brute force over subarray boundaries, since the tiny bound makes exhaustive checking acceptable and it's a yes/no existence question rather than an optimization
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: when the question is existence-of-a-specific-target under a tiny bound rather than the single best value, brute-force enumeration is a reasonable fit instead of the max/min-tracking DP.

items.prompt_md: Given a circular array of integers (the end wraps around to the start), find the maximum sum of any non-empty contiguous subarray, where the subarray may wrap around the end.
options: Run Kadane's twice — once normally for the max subarray, once inverted for the min subarray to derive the best wrapping sum — then take the better of the two
Track a running max and running min at each index, exactly like the product version
Two pointers converging from both ends of the circular array
Sort the array and sum the largest contiguous run
items.answer: Run Kadane's twice — once normally for the max subarray, once inverted for the min subarray to derive the best wrapping sum — then take the better of the two
items.explanation_md: The wraparound variant still rests on the same one-rolling-value Kadane idea, just applied twice (direct and inverted) to handle the circular case — no sign-flip tracking like the product version is needed since it's still a sum.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of integers, find the maximum absolute difference between the running max product ending at any index and the running min product ending at any later index — effectively you must recover BOTH extremes at every position, not just the overall best.
options: Track running max and running min at every index exactly as in max-product-subarray, storing both arrays instead of only the final answer
A single running max is enough, since only the overall best value across the whole array matters
Sort the array first to separate positive and negative runs
Use backtracking to enumerate every subarray pair
items.answer: Track running max and running min at every index exactly as in max-product-subarray, storing both arrays instead of only the final answer
items.explanation_md: This still needs the full running-max/running-min recurrence at every position — just keep both full arrays instead of collapsing to a single final answer.

items.prompt_md: You are given a fixed-size sliding window of the last k elements of a stream and must report the maximum product within that exact window at every step, discarding elements as they fall outside the window.
options: Maintain a deque of candidate extremes for the fixed window, since a plain rolling max/min pair can't 'forget' elements sliding out of a bounded window
Track a running max and running min exactly like unbounded max-product-subarray
Sort each window from scratch at every step
Two pointers converging from both ends of the window
items.answer: Maintain a deque of candidate extremes for the fixed window, since a plain rolling max/min pair can't 'forget' elements sliding out of a bounded window
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: once the subarray must fit inside a FIXED, sliding window (elements can expire), the unbounded running-max/min trick from this lesson no longer applies as-is — a window-aware structure is needed instead.
